findings.description: search_by_name interpolates the user-supplied fragment directly into the SQL string via f-string, enabling SQL injection.
reserve uses 'stock > quantity' so reserving exactly the available stock fails; should be '>=' (off-by-one).
reserve does not validate quantity > 0, so a negative quantity passes the check and adds stock while returning True.
reserve performs a non-atomic read-then-update (TOCTOU), so concurrent reservations can oversell stock.
low_stock_report issues one movements COUNT query per low-stock product inside the loop (N+1), instead of a single aggregated/joined query.
restock does not validate quantity > 0, allowing a negative value to silently reduce stock.
Even when parameterized, fragment is not escaped for LIKE wildcards ('%' and '_'), allowing unintended broad matches.
restock silently no-ops when the SKU does not exist (UPDATE affects zero rows) with no caller feedback.
Product(**row) couples the dataclass tightly to exact SELECT column names; a schema/column mismatch raises TypeError at runtime.
Connections are opened manually with try/finally rather than using sqlite3.connect as a context manager, which is more idiomatic and safer.
A module-level logger is created but never used anywhere in the file.
